summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is carved up by an implicit technical vs. non-technical divide, which is a false boundary imposed on a continuous spectrum of work. This gerrymandering distorts product design—especially in analytics consumption—by forcing tools into narrow buckets instead of enabling a unified experience across users and use cases.
money_quotes: The artificial boundaries we define eventually come to define us.
The line between BI reporting and analytical research isn’t a hard line; it’s a fluid, shifting continuum.

Like a city divided by a poorly drawn political boundary, our method for consuming data is split by an arbitrary technical line that makes it extraordinarily difficult to cross a border that we frequently need to cross.
The modern data stack doesn’t need a BI bucket and a data science bucket; it needs a unified consumption layer.
The sooner we see the space as a spectrum, and not a thousand discrete colors, the sooner we can build a better experience for everyone.
key_insight: Treating data work as a technical/non-technical dichotomy gerrymanders the stack and blocks the unified, spectrum-based tools teams actually need.
